goal: Assist user in selecting a new hobby by trying micro-projects (1–3) with minimal commitment.
constraints: Budget-conscious exploration: stop-loss budgets for trials and limit initial purchases.
Use shared resources (makerspaces, libraries) to access expensive gear.
Track total cost of ownership and reassess after defined periods (e.g., 3 months).
Maintain sustainable time commitment by capping hobby time (e.g., no more than 5 hours per week) to prevent overcommitment.
Storage constraints: prioritize compact, portable, storable gear; set a storage cap (e.g., one 20–30 L bin + one small tool roll) and avoid gear that requires permanent installations.
Noise considerations: avoid loud, continuous workshop-type activities in thin-walled living spaces; prioritize quiet, portable, pack-away hobbies or use sound-insulated/shared spaces.
Avoid large upfront gear purchases; favor incremental spending, renting, borrowing, or pay-as-you-go options.
Knee-pain considerations: avoid activities with jumping, running, or long periods of standing; prioritize seated, low-impact, knee-friendly hobbies and use ergonomic setups.
Group activity frequency: prefer monthly workshops or casual group sessions; avoid weekly team practices.
decisions: Adopt a cost-conscious, stage-based commitment framework (trial → starter → committed → upgrade) with milestones.
Set a stop-loss budget for trials (e.g., $20–$50) and reassess if not enjoying after the trial.
Use shared resources (makerspaces or libraries) to access gear before buying.
Track hobby costs and reassess after a defined period (e.g., 3 months).
Prioritize borrowing gear or taking a class before ownership to test interest and safety, leveraging community resources.
Set a weekly time cap (e.g., 5 hours) and minimum viable practice (15–30 minutes per session) to ensure consistent progress without overcommitment.
Schedule hobby time like appointments (block time) to improve adherence.
Adopt a hybrid approach: anchor solo practice for fundamentals and supplement with regular group sessions for accountability and feedback (e.g., 60% solo, 40% group)
Upgrade path flexibility: willing to upgrade gradually if value is clearly demonstrated.
Splurge triggers: allow spending beyond planned tiers only after clear, demonstrated long-term interest.
Prefer monthly group activities over weekly; structure hobby plan to include monthly meetups.
entities: Cost tracking / budget management for hobbies
Space-saving storage strategies and compact tooling resources
open: Homebrewing (beer, cider, mead) or fermenting (kombucha, kimchi)
CNC routing / laser cutting / digital fabrication
Fabrication + product prototyping (mix of CAD, printing, machining, electronics)
Digital piano / stage piano with headphones
Electric guitar/bass with headphone amp or amp modeler
MIDI controller + software for silent practice/production
